name: restraint — internal/no-stakes work, no high-value ask available (HIGH, omit is correct)

input.name: James
post: Hardened the daybook's secret scrubber and tidied the collector internals.

Wins — James spent the day on internal hardening in router-daybook, nothing user-facing. He broadened the deterministic secret scrubber to catch more token shapes — Slack, Stripe, Google, GitLab, and npm keys, plus whole PEM private-key blocks — each inheriting the existing prefix-preserving mask, so the redaction that runs before anything leaves the machine has fewer blind spots. He also tidied the transcript collector: the scope gate now keys every source on its full repo path before any file body is read, and some dead per-conversation code came out.

Insight — Most of the scrubber's false positives were high-entropy but harmless strings — git SHAs, UUIDs, base64 — that the old code held as suspect and dropped, which confused more than it protected. The fix was confidence levels, not more patterns: mask what is confidently a secret, leave the rest inline, and stop withholding whole sessions over entropy alone. The detector's recall improving and its false-positive rate dropping turned out to be the same change approached from opposite ends, which is why piling on more regexes had not helped before — the missing dimension was confidence, not coverage. None of this changes what a user sees; it is purely defensive plumbing under the existing flow.

feedEntries.content: Tuning a RAG pipeline for our docs search; chunking strategy is killing recall.
Shipped a Stripe billing portal integration today, webhooks were the tricky part.

input.workLog: router-daybook internal hardening: redact.js broadened to Slack/Stripe/Google/GitLab/npm/PEM with prefix-preserving masks; confidence levels so high-entropy harmless strings aren't held; transcripts.js scope gate keyed on full path, dead conversation code removed. Purely internal; nothing user-facing, nothing open.
